title: Resonant delocalization in Random Schroedinger operators on graphs of rapid volume growth
speaker: Michael Aizenmann

proof_stages.label: Random Schrödinger phase-setting
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sets up random Schrödinger operators H = T + λV on graphs or lattices, recalls the localization versus absolutely continuous spectrum phase picture, and frames the central issue as extended states produced by resonant local quasi-modes.

proof_stages.label: Rapid-growth motivation and ergodicity benchmark
proof_stages.summary: Tree-like and hypercube-like configuration spaces are motivated by many-particle systems, and Boltzmann/eigenfunction equidistribution is introduced as the heuristic principle that resonant extended states may nevertheless violate.

proof_stages.label: Regular-tree phase diagram theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the result with Warzel that the previously unresolved part of the regular-tree random Schrödinger phase diagram has absolutely continuous spectrum, including edge regimes where earlier intuition suggested localization.

proof_stages.label: Rare quasi-modes versus exponential volume
proof_stages.summary: A heuristic mechanism is developed: rare localized quasi-modes can resonate when their energy gap is smaller than the exponentially small tunneling amplitude, and on exponentially growing trees the number of possible partners can overcome the small probability, governed by Lyapunov exponents, large deviations, and second-moment considerations.

proof_stages.label: Resolvent definition of tunneling resonance
proof_stages.summary: Quasi-modes are formalized as approximate eigenpairs, tunneling amplitudes are defined through a two-dimensional projected resolvent and Schur-complement calculation, and the two-level matrix criterion |ΔE| less than or comparable to |τ| is identified as the rule for resonant delocalization.

proof_stages.label: Many-level and many-body limitations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the two-level test omits many-level interference and possible hierarchical resonance structures, connecting these issues to many-body localization and motivating simpler graph models as test cases.

proof_stages.label: Complete-graph test model
proof_stages.summary: A complete-graph random Schrödinger operator is introduced with rank-one hopping and scaled i.i.d. Gaussian potential, and its extreme-value spectrum is described through localized potential levels that undergo avoided crossings with the single extended rank-one state.

proof_stages.label: Complete-graph resonance windows and BGS limit
proof_stages.summary: Applying the resonance criterion via explicit level-spacing and Hilbert-transform computations identifies delocalization windows near 0 and -1, where the scaled eigenvalue process converges to the Bogomolny–Gerland–Schmit process and eigenfunctions are geographically delocalized while remaining L2-localized.

examples.name: Hypercube spin-chain configuration space
examples.note: The n-spin up/down configuration graph is a hypercube, illustrating why many-particle configuration spaces can resemble rapidly growing graphs without being used in the proof presented here.

examples.name: Gaussian Lifshitz-tail rare islands
examples.note: For a Gaussian potential at weak disorder, the speaker estimates an exponentially small density of high-potential islands to explain why edge states would naively look localized before resonances are considered.
examples.engagement_reason: he heuristically derives the Gaussian Lifshitz-tail scale by requiring potential values of order 1/lambda and estimating their Gaussian probability as exp(-const/lambda^2)

examples.name: Random energy model extreme-value analogy
examples.note: In response to a question about Gaussian maxima, the speaker compares the complete-graph potential extremes with the random energy model’s Poisson extreme-value statistics.
examples.engagement_reason: he works through the extreme-value estimate for independent Gaussian energies, solving m·P(G>v)≈1 to get the sqrt(2 log m) edge and Poisson-tip behavior
